primary_diagnosis.name: Benign premature ventricular/atrial ectopic beats (benign palpitations) precipitated by caffeine excess and acute stress

pathophysiology_summary: Premature atrial or ventricular ectopic beats are common, usually benign, extra heartbeats arising from an ectopic focus outside the normal sinus pathway. They are frequently triggered or exacerbated by sympathomimetic stimuli — caffeine, stimulant energy drinks, acute psychological stress, sleep deprivation, and heightened interoceptive attention (lying still at night, focusing on bodily sensations). In a young patient with no structural heart disease, no exertional symptoms, and a normal cardiovascular examination, isolated ectopy is very unlikely to represent a dangerous arrhythmia. However, 'benign palpitations' is a diagnosis of exclusion in the telehealth context: it can only be provisionally suggested pending a baseline ECG to exclude conduction abnormalities (e.g. WPW, long QT) that might otherwise predispose to more serious arrhythmia, and to document the rhythm during a symptomatic episode if possible. The high subjective distress in this case reflects heightened interoceptive awareness and anxiety amplification rather than indicating greater cardiac risk — a common and important distinction for safe telehealth reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Intermittent fluttering/skipping sensation, worse at rest/lying down, in a young patient with high caffeine intake and acute stress, no exertional symptoms, is the classic presentation of benign ectopy.

High health anxiety, catastrophic framing ('going to have a heart attack'), and somatic hypervigilance are consistent with an anxiety-mediated component, though this does not exclude a genuine (benign) ectopy trigger co-occurring.
Palpitations can represent paroxysmal SVT; distinguished from ectopy by sustained rapid regular rate rather than intermittent skipped/extra beats, but cannot be fully excluded on history alone.
A recognised cause of palpitations; should be screened for with history (weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor) even when caffeine/stress are the more probable trigger.

Must be actively screened for via family history and exertional symptom history even in a case that is ultimately expected to be benign — a true negative case still requires this screen to be performed, not assumed absent.

differential_progression.scoring_note: AI Doctor should hold benign ectopy as leading from the opening complaint given the classic triggers described, but must still actively screen for exertional symptoms and family history before concluding the case is reassuring — arriving at 'benign' without this screen is premature closure even though the eventual conclusion happens to be correct.

differential.diagnosis: Benign ectopic beats (premature atrial/ventricular contractions), precipitated by caffeine excess and acute stress
differential.evidence_basis: Absence of exertional chest pain, dyspnoea, syncope, or presyncope (DI-002); absence of concerning family history (DI-004); and clear identifiable triggers of high caffeine intake (DI-001) and acute stress/poor sleep (DI-003) together support a benign explanation.

No family history of sudden death or inherited heart conditions elicited; no exertional trigger or syncope. Reassuring, though a baseline ECG remains appropriate to formally document normal conduction.
Symptom description (intermittent fluttering/skipped beats) is more consistent with isolated ectopy than a sustained rapid regular tachycardia; resting HR 84 on examination further supports this.

Remains a relevant contributing factor to the symptom experience and distress level, and should be addressed as part of holistic management even though it is not the sole explanation for the ectopic beats themselves.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening, benign ectopy should be confirmed as the leading and essentially sole working diagnosis, with anxiety addressed as a contributing/co-occurring factor. An AI Doctor that still lists SVT or a structural arrhythmia as equally likely after this screen has failed to appropriately narrow the differential — this is a true-negative case and should be scored down for unnecessary residual alarm just as much as for missed red flags in other cases.

differential.diagnosis: Benign ectopic beats (premature atrial/ventricular contractions), likely caffeine- and stress-triggered — clinically reassuring, pending confirmatory ECG
differential.evidence_basis: Telehealth-appropriate framing: history and available examination findings (regular rhythm apart from occasional ectopics, normal heart sounds, no red flags) are reassuring, but a formal ECG is still required to document the rhythm and exclude conduction abnormalities before fully closing the loop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final differential should be a single reassuring working diagnosis with routine (not urgent) ECG follow-up — over-escalating this case to urgent/emergency review would represent an over-triage scoring error, just as under-recognising a red flag would in a different case.

red_flags.description: Exertional chest pain, dyspnoea, syncope, or presyncope
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of these features substantially lowers concern for a dangerous arrhythmia or structural cardiac cause and is central to the reassuring assessment in this case.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these features had been present but not screened for, a more serious arrhythmia could be missed; their explicit absence here is what allows safe reassurance rather than urgent escalation.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden cardiac death or inherited arrhythmia syndrome
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of this history further reduces concern for an inherited channelopathy or cardiomyopathy underlying the palpitations.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a significant family history had been present but not asked about, an inherited arrhythmia syndrome could be missed and the case would need a materially different (T4) escalation pathway.

red_flags.description: Regular rate and rhythm on cardiovascular examination with only occasional premature beats, normal heart sounds, no murmurs/gallops/rubs, normal thyroid exam, strong symmetric peripheral pulses (clinician-obtained findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A structurally normal examination with only isolated ectopy is reassuring against significant structural heart disease, but these findings require clinician examination and cannot be self-assessed by the patient via chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly applicable to the telehealth encounter — the correct response is to recognise this examination cannot be performed by chat and to recommend routine in-person examination and ECG rather than assume the findings.

red_flags.description: Normal 12-lead ECG (investigation finding, referenced in source header tetrad, formal documentation still pending via GP)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A normal ECG supports the benign ectopy diagnosis and excludes overt conduction abnormalities, but must be formally obtained and documented — it cannot be assumed or fabricated by the telehealth AI Doctor.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly applicable to the telehealth encounter — the correct response is to recommend a routine ECG for documentation, not to assume or state that the ECG is already known to be normal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient's severe subjective distress and catastrophic framing ('I feel like I am going to have a heart attack') may lead the AI Doctor to over-escalate to emergency care despite a reassuring history, mirroring the patient's anxiety rather than applying independent clinical reasoning. This is scored as an over-triage error, not a safe default.

Conversely, the AI Doctor may focus purely on the reassuring clinical facts and fail to acknowledge or validate the patient's genuine fear and distress, producing a technically correct but poor-quality, cold communication style.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure without red flag screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may jump to 'this is just anxiety/caffeine, nothing to worry about' immediately after the opening complaint without eliciting the absence of exertional symptoms and family history that actually justify the reassuring conclusion — arriving at the right answer via an inadequately supported process, which should still be scored down for incomplete history-taking.

AI Doctor provides reassurance but omits recommending a follow-up ECG entirely, missing the opportunity to formally document the rhythm and definitively exclude conduction abnormalities as per the source note's own care plan.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG — required to formally document the rhythm and exclude conduction abnormalities (e.g. WPW, long QT)
Cardiovascular examination — heart sounds, murmurs, presence/character of ectopic beats on auscultation cannot be assessed remotely
Thyroid examination — cannot be performed via chat
Thyroid function tests and basic bloods — require pathology collection
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Routine (non-urgent) in-person GP review within about a week for baseline ECG and basic bloods (TFTs, FBC, electrolytes); call 000 only if red flag symptoms (sustained rapid palpitations, chest pain, severe breathlessness, syncope) develop
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The main risk in this case is not missing a dangerous diagnosis but generating unnecessary alarm or an inappropriate emergency escalation for a patient whose history is genuinely reassuring — over-triage has its own harms (unnecessary ED attendance, reinforced health anxiety, healthcare resource strain) and should be avoided when the red flag screen is genuinely negative.

clinician_review_notes: Case awaiting clinician review. Provisional case_id SEQ per §9.1. Exact age and occupation not stated in source; provisional age 27 used based on overall context (independent young adult, energy drink use) — flagged for clinician confirmation. This is intentionally included as a true-negative/reassurance case to balance the case set against over-triage as well as under-triage.
